name: Binary Representation and Bit Tricks
tagline: Numbers in base 2 enable cheap tests (powers of 2 via x & (x-1)), fast multiplications (x*7 = (x<<3) - x), exact probability simulation from a fair coin (binary expansion of p), and parallel encoding (10 mice for 1000 bottles via 10-bit labels).

blocks.text: Bit-level identities solve interview classics in O(1). x & (x - 1) clears the lowest set bit; if the result is 0 and x > 0, then x is a power of 2. x * (2^k +/- 1) reduces to a shift plus add/subtract: x * 7 = (x << 3) - x; x * 9 = (x << 3) + x. These tricks were essential when multiplications were expensive, and they remain useful for embedded code and hot loops.

For probability simulation: any p in [0, 1) has a binary expansion p = 0.p_1 p_2 p_3 ... in base 2. Toss a fair coin repeatedly and read the toss as a bit s_i. If s_i < p_i, declare WIN; if s_i > p_i, declare LOSE; otherwise continue. The procedure terminates a.s. (geometric expected length) and yields P(WIN) = p exactly.

Parallel binary encoding: with k binary tests you can identify any of 2^k items. The poisonous-wine puzzle (1000 bottles, 10 mice) labels bottle b with its 10-bit binary representation; mouse i drinks every bottle whose i-th bit is 1; the dead-mice pattern reads off the poisoned bottle's index. This is essentially a parallel binary search.

blocks.label: Mental model
blocks.text: Binary is information theory in code: each bit answers one yes/no question. Tasks reduce dramatically when we can pose those questions in parallel (mice, indicators) or amortize a multiplication over a constant number of shifts.

whenToUse: Constant-time tests: power of 2, parity, even / odd.
Fast multiplication / division by constants on hardware without a multiplier.
Simulating arbitrary probabilities from a fair coin.
Parallel encoding for combinatorial puzzles or testing.
Compact storage / set membership via bitmasks.
recipe: Multiply by 2^k +/- c: shift then add/subtract by c units.
Probability p: read its binary expansion p_i; compare against fair-coin tosses.
Encode N items with ceil(log_2 N) bit-positions; assign each test to one bit.
Population count: Brian Kernighan's loop x &= x-1 each iteration counts set bits.
commonPitfalls: Forgetting (x > 0) in the power-of-2 test -- otherwise -1, 0 also pass.
Overflow from x << 3 when x is near INT_MAX.
Probability simulation: not realizing the expected number of tosses can be unbounded for irrational p (each step terminates with constant prob 1/2 -- expected 2 tosses).
Assuming the dead-mice pattern uniquely identifies a bottle without an explicit binary labeling step.
Confusing logical AND (&&) with bitwise AND (&) in C/C++.

appearsIn.title: Test if Integer is a Power of 2
